title: Beer bread
subtitle: A no-knead quick loaf leavened with beer and baking powder
excerpt: A rough-textured quick bread that needs no yeast, no kneading, and no proving. Beer provides the leavening alongside baking powder. Mix, pour, bake: it is in the oven in five minutes.
sourceNotes: Quick breads leavened with chemical agents are documented in British and American baking literature from the mid-nineteenth century following the commercial production of baking powder. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, includes several soda-raised loaves. Beer as a leavening supplement in quick breads is a traditional Irish and British practice, using the carbon dioxide and yeast activity in ale.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice and freeze once cooled. Toasts well from frozen.

glossaryTerms.definition: A bread leavened with chemical raising agents (baking powder or bicarbonate of soda) rather than yeast, requiring no rising time before baking.

content.text: with a coarser texture and more character than most. The beer (any ale or stout works) contributes both CO₂ and flavour. Baking powder provides additional lift. The two together give a reasonable crumb without any of the work of a yeasted loaf.

The loaf is dense and good, with a crackled top crust and a slightly chewy interior that slices well. It is best eaten on the day it is made, thickly sliced with salted butter and sharp Cheddar. A stronger beer gives a more pronounced flavour; a mild pale ale gives a gentler result. Avoid very hoppy beers, which can taste bitter when baked.

attrs.prepNote: melted, plus extra for the tin

content.text: Heat the oven to 190°C fan (210°C conventional). Butter a 900 g loaf tin. Whisk {{plain-flour}}, {{baking-powder}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{granulated-sugar}} together in a large bowl. Pour in 330 ml of beer (one standard can or bottle) and mix briefly with a wooden spoon until just combined. Do not overmix; a few lumps are fine. Pour the batter into the prepared tin and smooth the top. Drizzle {{unsalted-butter}} over the surface.

Bake for 40–45 minutes. The loaf is done when the top is deep golden-brown and cracked, the sides have pulled slightly from the tin, and a skewer inserted into the centre comes out clean. Do not rely on colour alone; check the internal temperature with a probe thermometer, which should read 90–93°C. Cool in the tin for 10 minutes, then turn out onto a rack.
Self-raising flour can replace plain flour and baking powder. Omit the baking powder entirely and use 375 g of self-raising flour.
For a cheese and herb variation: add 80 g of grated hard cheese and 1 tablespoon of finely chopped rosemary or thyme to the dry ingredients.
The butter drizzle is optional but creates a shiny, richer top crust. Wholegrain mustard brushed over the top in the last 10 minutes of baking is good with cheese variations.
